Name: latency-aware_pac-bayes_inference
Title: PAC-Bayes Meets Latency-Aware Inference: A Jacobian-Based Layer-Skipping Framework
Short Hypothesis: Can we derive a PAC-Bayes bound for latency-aware inference that allows for efficient layer-skipping in neural networks using Jacobian-based stability metrics, without compromising accuracy?
Related Work: Existing work on latency-aware inference focuses on heuristic methods or empirical pruning techniques, often lacking theoretical guarantees. PAC-Bayes frameworks have been applied to generalization bounds but not to latency-aware inference. Spectral stability and Jacobian-based methods have been explored separately but not in the context of layer-skipping for latency reduction. This proposal bridges these areas by introducing a theoretically grounded, latency-aware inference framework.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for latency-aware inference in neural networks that combines PAC-Bayes theory with Jacobian-based layer-skipping. The key idea is to derive a PAC-Bayes bound that ensures the stability of the network's hidden states when skipping layers, based on the spectral properties of the Jacobian matrix. This allows us to dynamically skip layers during inference while maintaining accuracy, thus reducing latency. We introduce a Jacobian-based stability metric that quantifies the perturbation caused by layer-skipping and use this to inform the PAC-Bayes bound. Our experiments demonstrate that this framework can significantly reduce inference latency without compromising accuracy, outperforming existing heuristic methods. We validate our approach on standard benchmarks, showing that it achieves a better throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to baseline methods.

Experiments.Description: Validate the PAC-Bayes bound on a standard convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on CIFAR-10.
Experiments.Procedure: Train a CNN on CIFAR-10, apply the Jacobian-based layer-skipping framework, and measure latency and accuracy. Compare with baseline methods.

Experiments.Description: Test the framework on a transformer model trained on a text classification task (e.g., GLUE).
Experiments.Procedure: Train a transformer model on a GLUE task, apply the layer-skipping framework, and measure latency and accuracy. Compare with baseline methods.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the impact of different Jacobian-based stability thresholds on latency and accuracy.
Experiments.Procedure: Vary the stability threshold and measure the trade-off between latency and accuracy.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The PAC-Bayes bound may be too conservative, limiting the practical benefits of layer-skipping.
The Jacobian-based stability metric may introduce computational overhead during inference.
The framework may not generalize well to all types of neural networks or tasks.
